Vor. Il FEBRUARY, 1911. No. 7

—_—
WILLIAM REEVES—BISHOP AND BIBLIOGRAPHER.

As a companion portrait to that of the book-loving; Bishop Murphy
(September, 1910), we now present a short sketch of another bishop,
also a County Cork man, Dr. Reeves, who was born at Charleville on
St. Patrick’s Eve, 1815. Educated at Geohegan’s celebrated academy in

- Hume Street, Dublin, he entered T.C.D. in October, 1830, and gradua-
ted B.A. in 1835. Being too young for ordination in the church—his
chosen profession, he turned his avtention to the study of medicine,
and took the M.B. degree. In 1838 he was ordained at Hillsborough, to
the curacy of Lisburn, in the diocese he was long afterwards called to
rule. Three years later, he exchanged this for the perpetual curacy of
- Kileonriola or Ballymena, to which he added the duties of headmaster
of the Diocesan school there. This, though engaged in hard work,

- poorly paid and despite domestic sorrow, he regarded as the happiest
time of his life, and here without bating one jot of his parochial or
scholastic duties, he found time to produce his first great work * The
Eeclesiastical Antiquities of Down, Connor and Dromore,” Dublin,

1847, which placed him at once in the front rank of anviquaries and
~ Scholars. This was followed in 1850 by his ‘“ Acts of Archbishop
Colton in his visitation of the Diocese of Derry in 1397 (4to., Dublin.)
o His next great work was his edition of Adamnan’s ‘‘ The Life of St.

- Columpyg, 7 (Dublin, 1857), admitted on all hands to be his master-
Picce, and received with a chorus of praise by scholars at home and
abrnad Its publication led an illustrious foreign scholar 1romcmlly to
'cmnphment the Irish Church upon its superabundant wealth of intel-

tance the sum of £300 to save the relic, whilst prelates and purse.
proud magnates stood coldly aloof. 1In his visits to the Royal Irig),
Academy (to which he was elected in 1846, and became presideny iy
1391), Reeves carried this precious burden in a leathern wallet slung
over h_is shoulder. The establishment of the first series of the * Ulstey
Journal of Archwmology » in 1852 afforded him a field for the publica-
tion of his wide local researches, and many valuable papers from his
pen, on antiquities, history and bibliography illuminate its pages, and
are well worthy of republicanioﬁ- After a sojourn of seventeen years ip
Ballymena, where after the lapse of half a century his name is sti]]
revered. Dr. Reeves was promoted to the vicarage of Lusk, Co. Dublin,
and as was his wont, immediately began to collect materials for a his.

tory of the parish, and repaired the round tower then falling into de-

cay from funds derived from the sale of his pamphlet ‘‘ The Ancient

Churches of Armagh,” (Lusk, 1860).

In 1861 the then Primate, Lord George Beresford, offered Reeves
the librarianship at Armagh, a congenial task which he readily accep-
ted. His first duty was to make out the catalogue which is still in use,
in two folios, in his neat handwriting, recalling the penmanship of the

-ancient scribes, whose works he loved to pore over and translate, ‘‘ The

duties of this position,’”’ we can well believe, ‘‘ were delightful to him;
he loved the books, and to catalogue them was his greatest enjoy-
ment.” Another friend refers to ‘‘ his amazingly minute and full
When the
Library Association met in Dublin in 1884, Dean Reeves, as he had
then become, read a paper entitled ‘“ A Memoir of the Public Library

catalogue of the hooks, and of their important contents.”

of Armagh,” of the greatvest interest, published by the Chiswick Press,

1886. He and Henry Bradshaw were the guests of Dr. J. K. Ingram—

| :--"a, dellghtful trio. Within a few short months Bradshaw was no more:

friends- gam IFerguson wrote a congratulatory poem, but the book-

ppensated by the primacy.” But the higher honour was not

o he his, for in June, the Bishop of Down was elevated, and

¢ o wrench ”’ to give up his library, ‘‘ a sacrifice, only o

Reeves unaninio
can well pity your suffering heart in the severance from Armagh, and

jot least from the host of standard old friends in the bookshelves of 1its
woble library.”  He ruled over the united diocese well and wisely until

usly appointed to the vacancy. One friend writes: ** L

his death on 12th January, 18902. No more notable worker in the field
of Trish antiquities and bibliography ever lived than William Reeves—
flis motto was ‘‘Thorough,”” and no one was more willing to place his
great knowledge at the disposal of every inquirer. He assisted Elrington
and Cotton, hesides a host of other writers, as innumerable works tes-
tify. A horn book-lover—from his earliest days he exhibited a wonderful
knowledge of hooks gained in the library of his grandfather, Caprain
Roberts, and his tastes were first given an antiquarian bent by the
perusal of the old ‘¢ Dublin Penny Journal.” His wife once said to him :
“I like to see you amongst your books! you look so happy,” and writ-
ing to John O’Donovan in reference to a MS. in the great Stowe sale,
he said: ‘“ To procure that MS. I would live on ‘ stirabout’ as many
months as there are pages in the book!”” As an amusement he cata-
logued the library of the University Club, Dublin, and he was practic-
ally the compiler of the * Lough Fea Catalogue,”’ which he enriched
With copious notes. :

His ¢ Life”’ has Dbeen written with sympathy and knowledge by

L'ad}’ Ferguson (Dublin, 1893), to which is appended a bibliography of
his multifarious writings, by J. Ribton Garstin, a model of what such
2 compilation should be, of which 100 copies were privately printed.

The years thav have fled since Reeves" death have naturally thin-

ned the ranks of his contemporaries and co-workers. But amongst ‘the
Wvivors, Dr, Norman Moore, the scholarly physician, who wrote the

"0tice in D.N.B., kindly supplied us' with the following 1_‘90011551“&0118 of .

known a modern instance of the same kind at the time of the Tractarigy

Tract No. 90 The
bookseller, after some delay, wrote: °Sir, I regret that though I gy,

acy

oriental saying (it was in relation to ancient earthworks in Irelang) .
‘“ Three eagles one tortoise; three tortoises one furrow ; three furrows
the world.”” He did all he could to prevent the ploughing-up or njury
of forts, moats, raths and lisses. A farmer in Co. Down would dig 1mtg
a work of this kind in spite of Dr. Reeves’ remonstrances. A fey
-months later the inroad had obviously been discontinued and Reeves
said, ‘I am glad you have stopped!” ¢ Well,’ “ when we
found a knee-cap among the potatoes we had dug there and were gomg

to eat it, we thought it was time to stop!’

sald the farmer,

It was by his intervention
that the Navan fort, near Armagh, the Eamhain of the old kings of
Ulster was saved from destruction. It was held on a peculiar perpetu-
ally renewable lease, and he pointed out that a condition in this docu-
ment adverse to the perpetual tenant’s tenure might he enforced under
the circumstances. The tvenant, who had meant to dw 1t down, under-
took not to injure the fort and thus this famous monument was pre-
served. I remember how pleased Dr. Reeves was, when on hearing of

this from him as we walked out together to the Navan fort, I said, that

“ he might justly say of himself with Cuchulain :—

‘ Nipsa chusa. ingaire gambma,
Basa chusa ingaire Eamhna.’’ _
(I was not a hound for watching calves

-~ I was a hound for watching Eamhain).

PRINTING IN TUAM, 1774-1825.

The following list of printing in Tuam 1is, I think, of interest, cover-
ing as it does the same period as the previous lists. One thing imme-
diately strikes the eye in reading iv, viz., the number of newspapers that
appeared there. There appears to have been greater appreciation of
pfess literature in some of our country towns at that period than one
would expect, but then 1t must be remembered that some of Tthese
papers had very short lives and also that comparatively few numbers
1c¢ survive. Sometimes a paper changed its name and resumed life
ander a different designation :(—

K. R. Mc. C. DIX.
THE ZIMMER LIBRARY.

University Co-llege, Dublin, like its London namesake, has been
fortunate encugh to acquire a great Celtic library, formed in this case
by the late Professor Zimmer, during the past thirty years. It 1
divided into seven sections of which the largest is that devoted to tho
language and literature of Ireland. This comprises 679 entries, but
many of these entries refer, not to single books or pamphlets, but ¥
sets of volumes. Thus a work in twenty volumes counts merely as 0%
entry. A great part of this section necessanly duplicates much al-
ready in the library, but the other sections supply a ‘much-needed

collection of Celvic material, all the most notable works 111 Irish

Ar chmology and hagiology are represented, whilst grammars “and dic
tionaries abound as well as all the more important texts and facsim

1les

and sets of Irlsh ]ournals. But above all Zimmer has abuﬂdﬂﬂﬂf

& annota.ted many of the volumes with his latest discoveries, and
margms and mter-leawes has written his accumulated stores 0
- o

OBITUARY.

Dr. S. H. BurcaEer, M.P.
of this estimable gentleman and brilliant scholar on 29th December,

—We record with much regret the death
at the age of 60. The son of Professor Butcher, afterwards Bishop
of Meath, he was educated at Marlborough and Trinity, Cambridge,
and became Senior Classic in 1873, succeeding to a fellowship the fol-
lowing year. He vacated this in 1876 by his marriage with Rose,
youngest daughter of Archbishop Trench, a lady of exceptionally
attractive character and accomplishment, but was immediately elected
to a similar position at Oxford, where his house became a social centre
for old and young. In 1882 he succeeded Blackie in the chair of Greek
at Edinburgh, The death

of his wife and his own indifferent health constrained him to retire,

where he remained for twenty-one years.

and the remainder of his life was spent in London, or at his charming
Irish home, Danesfort, Killarney. He took an active palt in politics,
served on several Royal Commissions on Education, and in 1906 was

returned unopposed for the University of Cambridge. In Parliament

be spoke with a precision of phrase and a delicate choice of English

fich as few could attain.
him by the unaffected frankness and sweetness of hlS manner He was
a member of the Il‘lSh Literary
SOCIB'Gy from its inception, and a constant reader of I.B.L. He pubhshed

In conversation he fascm&ted all who met

n conjunction with Andrew Lang the well-known translation of The
Odyssey in 1789 Demosthenes, 1881 ; Some aspects of the Greek
Gemus 1891 Anstotles Theory of Poetry, 1895; an edition of Demcs—- o
theneg: m 2 vols., 1903-7 and his Harvard Lectures on GleeL Sub;ects,- *

He was for long dramatic adviger
at. Drury Lane Theatre, and his fistic encounter with the eccentrio
painter, Whistler, afforded food for the gossips for many days.

ALEC O’DoxNoGHUE, who, under the pen name of ‘‘ Hajad,” had
contributed some fine verses to ‘‘ The Daily Chronicle,” passed away
peacefully, within hail of the new year. His verses were written from
a sick bed, to which he had long been confined, butv there was nothing
of. the querulous invalid abouv them, but rather a cheery gaiety, the

ofispring of a mind at peace.
WALSH OR CALLAGHAN?

A crisis in the struggle in Ireland was reached early in 1648. (On
the 20th of May, the Supreme Council of the Confederate Catholics
agreed to a cessation of arms with Inchiquin, and adherents .to thi
truce were excommunicated by Rinuccini, the Papal Nuncio. 'The
Council, with Peter Walsh atv its head, repudiated this measure and

appealed to Rome, an episode which caused considerable controversy in

Ireland. @ A work on this subject, * Vindiciarum Catholicorum
Hiberniae ad Alitophilum libri duo,”’ was condemned in 1652. Written
under the pseudonym of Philopater Irenaeus, it is atvributed in the
catalogue of the Grenville Library and by Brunet to Richard Bellings,
and a cross-reference to his name is in the Roman Index. Bellings wa
the secretary of the Irish Confederate Catholics, and was sent to Romé

to interview Pope Innocent X. He was undoubtedly in the thick of the

‘controversy, and was very probably the real author of the defenc n
question. By other authoritidg, however, Father Callaghan is . g0id.
to be responsible for the work, and it appears under his name in ‘.‘hﬁ

Bntlsh Museum Catalogue—Frank Hamel in ‘‘ The lera;ry

Maurice satisfactorily answered, and protestantism defended in seven
letters, etc., with Babylon fallen a poem in thirty-two stanzas, by
Daniel McAfee, Wesleyan minister, Cork, printed for the author, 1839,
8vo., pp- 72. In the ‘* Gentleman’s Magazine ’’ for March, 1832, pp.
958-9, are some verses by him, entitled ‘“ Where is she?’’ which I some-
times fancy many have prompted the words of Joseph Archer’s cele-
brated song ‘‘ Alice where art thou?’’ 1 have lately looked at MoAfee’s
last book in which there is a good portrait of the old man. It is ‘‘ The
pillar and ground of the truth,” published in 1872. The copy in the
British Museum Library is unthumbed and as clean as when issued from
the press thirty-eight years ago; five hundred pages of controversial
theology! He wrote several other books published at Cork, Belfast,
Dublin, etc., the first in the British Museum Library, which ha ‘ten

~ of his books, is dated 1823, the last 1872. When the son came to London

he altered the spelling of his name to Macafee, wisely, I think. Like
other briefless barristers he did a good deal of journalistic work, but

~although clever, he lacked the steadiness and patient perseverance of

his father.

I do nov know if there is any value in these three notes about my
old frlends, Davin, Molloy and Macafee, but if there 1s their pubhca-
tion is due to ¢ The Irish Book Lover,” for they weul_d never have been
Written but for that magazine.  RALPH THOMAS.

I am pleased to see « new novel ‘“ The Mating of Anthea ’ (Johy
Long) by that charming writer Miss Arabella Kenealy, indicating as it
does that her health, which some time ago gave cause for anxiety to
her. friends is now quite restored. This, I think, is the first important
work from her once prolific pen since the fine biography of her father—
the scholar and lawyer, Dr. Kenealy, M.P. The only fault I had to-
find with that work was that Miss Kenealy did not use a tithe of the
material in her possession.

The MSS. left by her father comprise letters from all the great
Iiterary lights of the Mid-Victorian era. Amongst them are the rejected
sheets of the famous biography of Maginn in the ‘“ Dublin University |
Magazine *’ of 1844, the rejection of which led to a challenge and almost
a duel between Kenealy and Lever, the then editor. Another cherished
relic is a lock of grey hair that once adorned the brow of
‘“ bright broken Maginn.”’

before a sufficient number of monographs are written on
periods to enable Irish history to be written as it ought. Such mono-
graphs may be expected as one result of the new Irish University.”

obscure branch of Numismatics. It has evidently heen g labour o
love, and the three years’ occupied in collecting materials ang elucj.
dating the various problems, have been well spenv. The authgy has
spared no effort to render his list of these coins, full and complete,
Besides consulting all the older authorities, from Simon and Snelling
to Lindsay, of Cork many of whose statements he corrects—he hgg
personally inspected the treasures of the British Museum and R.IA.
as well as the scientific storehouses of Stockholm, Copenhagen, ang
Helsingfors. In addition to his own fine collection he has laid those of
his friends and colleagues under contribution, with the happiest re.
sults.  The ten plates, illustrating 242 of the coins described, aro
well and clearly reproduced, and most helpful to a complete under-
standing of an abstruse—though interesting subject, on which, we feel
assured, this handsome volume, will, in time to come, be regarded as

the standard authority.

POST-BAG.

BrLow’s BiBLE.—When the late John Anderson was compiling his
« Catalogne of Early Belfast Printed Books,” 1890, he traced and com-
pared thirteen copies of the Bible printed by James Blow in Belfast 1n
MDCCLI. 1 have recently come across another copy, which was
offered to me by a dealer as a rare edition of MDCCII. On examining
the date closely with a magnifying glass, I found an abrasion of the
L converting it into I. In other respects the book was an imperfect
copy of the 1751 edition. A similarly doctored copy was palmed off on
the late DDr. R. R. Madden, and for long considered a unique copy of
an eaivion printed in 1702. It was bought by the late Lavens Ewart
and now rests in the Linen Hall Library, Belfast. |
Dundonald. | | DAVID KENNEDY.

of two wood blocks which he says were used in printing it, but they arg
1dentical with those used in the 1751 edition. He relates the fouowing
anecdote which he heard from Blow’s grandson, which should give the
quietus to the fable of the ** Sin on More Bible.” ‘¢ A passage Occurg
In a verse of one of the chapters of Isaiah—‘Sin no more.’ JIp the
hurry of printing the compositor transposed the letters of the word
'no,” so that the passage read * Sin on more ’ ; nor was the error gjg.
covered until a number of copies were issued and bound ; thereby cays.
ing considerable expense, as the sheets containing the mistake (up-
wards of 8,000 in number), had all to be cancelled and new ones prin
ted.” It is a well-known fact that the first Bible printed in Irelang,
was the work of Aaron Rhames of Dublin in 1714. TYPO.
Irise CHAP BoOKS.—As mentioned at p. 113, Vol. 1, the name “Chap
Buook ’’ appears to have been first applied about 1824. Previous to that
date they were known as ‘‘ Burton’s ’’—perhaps from a printer or book-
seller, who specialised in that class of work—a term I have seen usod
In quite recent catalogues. A writer in *“ The Dublin and Loauon
Magazine ’’ for August, 1825, says: ‘‘ The number of sixpenny or Bur-
ton books annually sold, was formerly immense. Four booksellers in
Dublin used to deal exclusively in them, and one had four presses con-
stantly employed, and published on an average fifty thousand annually;
besides these there were presses in Cork and Limerick employed on no
other work. It was supposed that in this way three hundred thousand
were every year printed and circulated. They were a principal com-
modity of hawkers and pedlars; and in every country town they were
exposed on tables in the streets for sale, and the markets resembled
a Leipsic fair, . . These publications are, however, now discontin-
ued. Mr. Parnell stated before the Commissioners of Revenue that ‘ the
common mode of getting books in Ireland is through the means Of
grocers and hardwaremen ; they keep a shelf for what they call Burton’s,
and spelling books, and almanacks.” ”’ BmLIO'

the poor hdﬁsé of Be’lfast, aged 90, Mr. Henry Linn, the oldest book-
‘binder probably in Ireland.” D5 J. O’D-"

odition of 1845, the third part of which contains six more pages than

DuBLiN PuBLIsHERS 1N 1720 (Vol. 11, p. 93).—Patrick Dugan and
Joseph L'eathly (or Leatly, or Leathley) were hooksellers in Dublin in
1720-21. They published in that year (besides Dr. South’s Sermons)
a third edition of Lord Clarendon’s * History of the Grand Rebellion
Compleated,” etec., and Rd. Cumberland’s * De Legibus Naturae.” I
know of no particular reason why they published South’s Sermons, but
publishing and printing were developing in Dublin then, and it was

‘usual to reprint there, any work esteemed in England, and at all likely

to. ﬁnd sale in Dublin. Dugan’s name is found in 1723 and 1724. He
was not a prmter Rea, . R, Mc. C. DIX.

IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY.

On the 12th January, Sir Alexander Lawrence, Bart., gave a very

esting lecture on *° The Lawrences in India.”” Many members of

;TjizlUIster family have served in India, hut the lecturer confined his
qccount to Captain Alexander Lawrence, who fought av the siege of
eringapatam, and his three famous sons John (afterwards Lord Law-
rence), Henry, and George, the first an Indian Civilian, the two others
officers in the Easv India Company’s Army. The three boys were
educated at Ifoyle College, Londonderry, and it is interesting to find
that when John and Henry Lawrence became members of the Board of
three, which administered the Punjab after ivs annexation, their collea-
gue, Sir Robert Montgomery, was an old schoolfellow. Talk of the old
school led them to send a handsome present one Christmas to an old
usher. In due course they received a pathetic letter of thanks from
the old gentleman, whom the timely gift had found in great poverty,
saying that he had searched in the school atlas, but had been unable
v discover Lahore, or even the Punjab. After the first Burma War,
Henry came home on sick leave, and worked in the Ordnance Survey of
Ireland, thus acquiring technical knowledge which. was of great service
in his Indian career. After these two years in Ireland he was never
again out of India except for the brief period of eight months. He and

his brother John each married an Irish wife. The first Afgh*m War

brought stirring adventures to George Lawrence, who was ﬁrst 2 Pris
oner and afterwards a hostage with the Afghans.

him a5 § hostage. The lecturer gave a very clear summary of the

nd Henry (whose dwergence of views had never led to personal dis-

On the 21st, Mr. J. H. Skrine lectured on *‘ The Irish Brigades iy
the Service of France.”” The chair being taken by Sir J. Digges I
Touche, K.C.S.1. Sketching the origin of the Brigade from the Treaty
of Limerick, when Sarsfield carried over to Ifrance all the soldiers of
James II., who refused to swear allegiance to William III., the lecturer
gave a clear and interesting summary of its campaigns 1n the Nether-
lands, Spain, Italy and Germany. The imposition of the Penal Laws
in Ireland sent a steady stream of ‘‘ Wild Geese ”’ to swell the ranks
of the Brigade through the first half of the 18th century. The num-
ber and names of the regiments varied, but five regiments were formed
under the titles of Mountcashel, Dillon, O’Brien (commanded by Lord
Clare), Dublin, and Athlone. The command of these regiments re-
mained with the exiled Irish gentlemen, and the uniform was always
scarlet, with a badge of the harp and crown, as it had been when the
nucleus of this future French force consisted of King James’ Guards.
Mr. Skrine described the brilliant feat of arms at Cremona when 600
Irishmen, under Major O’Mahony, repulsed a large Austrian force (in
which other Irish exiles were fighting) and saved the town for Irance.
In the campaigns of Marlborough, the Irish Brigade earned laurels ab
~ Blenheim, Ramillies (whereLord Clare fell with 37 of his officers and
366 of his men) and Malplaquet. In each of these French defeats the
Irish regiments preserved good order, but in 1745 at Ifontenoy, they
turned the scale, and, after one of the best contested battles in Turo-
pean history, drove back Cumberland’s forces. Mr. Skrine gave an
admirable account of this famous battle, illustrated by a plan, "
exhibited two most interesting contemporary prints. At the Revolu-
tion most of the Irish oﬁ‘iceré, Royalists and Catholic to the core, fol-
 lowed the Bourbons into exile, as a century hefore they had followed
the Stuarts. Mr. Skrine’s lecture was a model of compression without
